questions.q: Who flew to the Himalayas to fetch the life-saving herbs in this episode?
questions.explanation: Hanuman, renowned for his power of flight, was sent to the Himalayas to bring the healing herbs.

questions.q: For whom was Hanuman seeking the healing herbs?
questions.explanation: Lakshmana lay gravely wounded, and the herbs were needed to revive and heal him.

questions.q: When Hanuman could not identify the correct herb, what did he do?
questions.explanation: Unable to distinguish the right herb, Hanuman lifted and carried the whole mountain back to the battlefield.

questions.q: In which mountain range did Hanuman search for the herbs?
questions.explanation: The medicinal herbs grew on a peak in the lofty Himalayan mountains.

questions.q: Hanuman is the son of which deity?
questions.explanation: Hanuman is the son of Vayu, the wind god, which gives him his power of flight.

questions.q: Where was Lakshmana lying wounded when Hanuman went for the herbs?
questions.explanation: Lakshmana had fallen on the battlefield during the war against Ravana in Lanka.

questions.q: What special quality made the herbs visible on the mountain at night?
questions.explanation: The healing herbs glowed luminously, which is why the mountain is described as glowing.

questions.q: How did Hanuman transport the mountain back to the battlefield?
options: He carried it through the air
He floated it on the ocean
He had it pulled by chariots
questions.explanation: Hanuman flew through the air carrying the entire mountain in his hand.

questions.q: Against whom was Rama's army fighting when Lakshmana was wounded?
questions.explanation: The battle was the great war between Rama's forces and the demon king Ravana of Lanka.

questions.q: Hanuman is most famous in the Ramayana for his devotion to whom?
questions.explanation: Hanuman is celebrated as the supreme devotee of Rama, serving him with unwavering loyalty.

questions.q: Which Himalayan mountain is traditionally named as the one bearing the healing herbs?
questions.explanation: The herb-bearing peak is traditionally identified as Dronagiri, also called Mount Drona.

questions.q: Which physician of the monkey army named the herbs needed to heal Lakshmana?
questions.explanation: Sushena, the vanara physician, identified the medicinal herbs required to revive Lakshmana.

questions.q: What wound had brought Lakshmana to the brink of death before this errand?
options: A powerful weapon hurled by Indrajit
questions.explanation: Lakshmana was struck down by a mighty weapon (the Shakti) of Ravana's son Indrajit (Meghanada).

questions.q: Which of these is one of the four herbs traditionally said to grow on the mountain?
questions.explanation: Sanjivani, the herb that restores life, is the most famous of the herbs sought on the mountain.

questions.q: According to tradition, why did the herbs hide themselves when Hanuman arrived?
options: They concealed themselves to test or elude him
questions.explanation: Tradition says the herbs hid or concealed their glow, so Hanuman could not single them out and took the whole peak.

questions.q: Who urged or sent Hanuman on the mission to bring the herbs?
options: Sugriva and the army leaders, on Rama's behalf
questions.explanation: The vanara leaders, acting for the grieving Rama, dispatched Hanuman to fetch the healing herbs.

questions.q: In many tellings, who was Hanuman's mother?
questions.explanation: Hanuman's mother was Anjana, which is why he is also called Anjaneya.

questions.q: What is a common name for the kind of beings, like Hanuman, who made up Rama's army?
questions.explanation: Hanuman was a vanara, a member of the monkey-like race that formed Rama's allied army.

questions.q: Why was speed so critical to Hanuman's mission for the herbs?
options: Lakshmana had to be healed before sunrise
questions.explanation: The remedy had to be applied before daybreak, so Hanuman raced against time to return.

questions.q: Hanuman's power to grow vast in size and fly comes from which source in tradition?
options: A boon and his nature as Vayu's son
questions.explanation: Boons from the gods and his birth as the wind god's son gave Hanuman his powers of flight and immense size.

questions.q: After the herbs healed Lakshmana, what did Hanuman do with the mountain in many versions?
options: Carried it back and set it in its original place
questions.explanation: In many retellings Hanuman dutifully returned the mountain to its place in the Himalayas afterward.

questions.q: The episode of fetching the herbs occurs in which book (kanda) of Valmiki's Ramayana?
questions.explanation: The war episodes, including the herb-fetching, belong to the Yuddha Kanda, the book of war.

questions.q: In the Valmiki Ramayana, how many medicinal herbs does Sushena name as needed for the wounded?
questions.explanation: Sushena names four great herbs, including the life-restoring and wound-healing plants of the mountain.

questions.q: Between which two Himalayan peaks is the herb-mountain said to lie in Valmiki's account?
questions.explanation: Valmiki places the herb-bearing peak between mounts Rishabha and Kailasa in the Himalayas.

questions.q: On his flight, in a popular version Hanuman is attacked by which figure who mistakes him for a demon?
questions.explanation: In a well-known telling, Bharata, guarding Ayodhya, shoots the airborne Hanuman, mistaking him for a rakshasa.

questions.q: In some traditions, which demon was sent by Ravana to delay Hanuman during this herb-fetching journey?
questions.explanation: The demon Kalanemi, in several traditions, was dispatched by Ravana to obstruct and delay Hanuman.

questions.q: Which of these is NOT one of the four herbs traditionally associated with the mountain?
questions.explanation: Vishalyakarani, Sanjivani, and Savarnyakarani are named herbs; a separate fruit of immortality is not among them.

questions.q: The herb Vishalyakarani named in the episode had what specific power?
options: Extracting embedded weapons and closing wounds
questions.explanation: Vishalyakarani was the herb that drew out embedded arrows or weapons and healed the wound.

questions.q: Which mountain did Hanuman lift, according to the most common tradition naming the peak?
questions.explanation: By the most common tradition, Hanuman bore back Dronagiri, the mountain bearing the glowing herbs.

questions.q: From which mountain did Hanuman traditionally launch his great leaps and flights toward the Himalayas and Lanka?
questions.explanation: Hanuman is described launching his mighty flights from Mount Mahendra in the Ramayana.
